title: Rhubarb Crumble
subtitle: Sharp forced rhubarb under a butter and oat topping
excerpt: Rhubarb cut into short lengths, sugared and baked under a generous rubbed-in topping of butter, flour, oats, and brown sugar. The rhubarb collapses to a sharp, jammy compote while the topping bakes to a pale gold.
sourceNotes: Rhubarb crumble is a post-war British classic. The crumble topping became standard in British home cooking during the 1940s as a flour-and-fat substitute for pastry when shortening was scarce. This version follows the standard proportions from mid-twentieth-century British cookery writing.

content.text: Forced rhubarb, the pale pink stalks pulled in darkness from January to March, is sweeter and more tender than outdoor rhubarb. Either works for crumble. The difference is in how much sugar you need: forced rhubarb wants 80 g, outdoor rhubarb wants 120 g or more depending on how late in the season it was cut. The method is the same.

The topping here uses a proportion of rolled oats alongside the flour, which gives it more texture and a nuttier flavour than an all-flour crumble. Cold butter rubbed in by hand or pulsed in a food processor to the size of small breadcrumbs is correct. Don't try to rub it so finely that it disappears, some variation in size is what gives the finished crumble its character.

items.name: Rhubarb
items.prepNote: trimmed and cut into 3 cm lengths

content.text: A 28 × 20 cm baking dish or similar deep ovenproof vessel works well. The fruit layer should be at least 4 cm deep.

Heat the oven to 190°C fan. Toss {{rhubarb}} of rhubarb with {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar in the baking dish and set aside for 10 minutes while you make the topping. The sugar will draw moisture from the rhubarb; this becomes the syrup the fruit cooks in.

Put {{plain-flour}} of plain flour and {{light-brown-sugar}} of light brown sugar into a large bowl. Add {{unsalted-butter}} of cold cubed butter and rub into the flour between your fingertips, working quickly so the butter doesn't melt, until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs with some pea-sized lumps still visible. Stop rubbing before it becomes uniform, the variation in texture is what gives the crumble its character.

Scatter the crumble topping in an even layer over the rhubarb without pressing it down. Bake for 35, 40 minutes until the topping is pale gold and the rhubarb is bubbling at the edges. Rest for 5 minutes before serving. Serve with crème anglaise, custard from a tin, or cold double cream.

items.fix: Bake at 190°C fan and scatter the topping loosely without pressing. If still pale at 40 minutes, increase to 200°C for a further 5 minutes.
items.cause: Oven temperature was too low, or the topping was pressed down too firmly
items.symptom: Topping is pale and sandy rather than golden

items.fix: Macerate the rhubarb with the sugar for 20 minutes, then drain off the excess liquid before adding the topping. Use the drained syrup to serve alongside.
items.cause: The rhubarb was very juicy and released too much liquid during baking
items.symptom: Rhubarb is watery and the crumble has gone soggy

items.fix: Bake until the topping is properly golden, pale beige is underdone. A properly baked crumble topping smells nutty.
items.cause: Underbaked; the flour in the topping needs full heat to cook through

items.fix: Pre-cook the rhubarb in the dish with the sugar for 10 minutes before adding the topping, then bake as normal.
items.cause: Outdoor rhubarb cut in late summer can be quite tough; it may need longer cooking
items.symptom: Rhubarb is still fibrous after baking

content.text: The crumble topping arrived in British cooking during the Second World War as a quicker alternative to pastry that used less flour. Rhubarb was one of the first fruits paired with it, because the plant thrives in British gardens with almost no tending. The combination has been a fixture of home cooking ever since. The brief forced season runs January to March; outdoor stalks come from April. Forced rhubarb has brighter colour, sharper acid, and softer flesh. Either works in this dish; forced rhubarb just needs less sugar.
